title: The most faithful Odyssey
gist: A roundup of a landmark 1968 Italian TV Odyssey praised for its fidelity to Homer ahead of Christopher Nolan's condensed retelling, alongside a new Sueye Park album reviving Karl Goldmark's obscure violin concerto and critic David Thomson's likely final book, an unkind history of cinema for Donald Trump.

insights.claim: The 1968 RAI television adaptation L'Odissea, directed by Franco Rossi and running roughly nine hours across eight episodes, is considered among the most faithful screen adaptations of Homer's Odyssey and drew an audience of up to 16 million viewers on its original broadcast.
insights.speaker: Paul Lay
insights.so_what: Sets a fidelity and popularity benchmark against which new Odyssey adaptations, including Nolan's, are being measured.
insights.locator: When L'Odissea, the Italian TV series

insights.claim: Each episode of L'Odissea opened with the poet Giuseppe Ungaretti reading passages of the original verse, a device the author holds up as a benchmark of textual fidelity against which Christopher Nolan's upcoming, 'rather more condensed,' Odyssey blockbuster will be compared.
insights.speaker: Paul Lay
insights.so_what: Frames upcoming big-budget Odyssey adaptations as likely to trade textual fidelity for runtime compression.
insights.locator: An especially nice feature is that each one of the episodes

themes.theme: New releases in music and film criticism

insights.claim: Violinist Sueye Park's album Goldmark and Sibelius: Works for Violin and Orchestra revives Karl Goldmark's largely unknown Violin Concerto, a work blending Slavonic dance rhythms with German Romanticism reflecting Goldmark's Austro-Hungarian background, and pairs it with lesser-known Sibelius pieces rather than his famous violin concerto.
insights.speaker: Paul Lay
insights.so_what: Flags a notable recovery of an obscure but historically significant Austro-Hungarian violin work for classical-music followers.
insights.locator: The brilliant young Korean violinist Sueye Park

insights.claim: Critic David Thomson, described as 'the Dr Johnson of film critics,' is publishing what may be his final book at age 85, A Sudden Flicker of Light: a Revisionist History of the Movies, which the author flags as notably unkind to Donald Trump, quoting Thomson's line that Trump has 'despaired at the idea of seeing.'
insights.speaker: Paul Lay
insights.so_what: Notes a prominent critic's likely capstone work takes an explicit political swipe at Trump within a history of cinema.
insights.quote: despaired at the idea of seeing
insights.locator: Its conclusion is not kind to Donald Trump
